questions.question: Why is one slot left unused in a typical array-based implementation of a circular queue? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: To create an unambiguous distinction between the full and empty states
If front equals rear after an operation, it could mean the queue is empty (initial state) OR full (only one element)
By sacrificing one slot, when front equals rear we know the queue has exactly one element (full), while both equal -1 indicates empty
Example: In a queue of capacity 5, storing 4 elements with front=0 and rear=3 allows clear differentiation from the empty state

questions.question: Explain the operations of enqueue and dequeue in a circular queue with a suitable example. Draw diagrams to show changes in front and rear pointers.
keyPoints: Enqueue: Check if full using (rear+1)%capacity == front; If empty, set both front and rear to 0; Otherwise, use circular increment: rear = (rear+1)%capacity
Dequeue: Check if empty using front==-1 && rear==-1; Store array[front]; If front==rear (single element), reset both to -1; Otherwise, use circular increment: front = (front+1)%capacity
Show step-by-step trace with at least one wrap-around operation
Include isEmpty and isFull conditions in explanation
Handle special cases: first insertion sets both pointers to 0; last dequeue resets both to -1

questions.question: Compare and contrast linear queue and circular queue implementations. What are the advantages of circular queues?
keyPoints: Linear queue wastes memory after dequeuing (wasted front indices); circular queue reuses vacated slots immediately
Linear queue full condition: rear == MAXSIZE-1; Circular uses (rear+1)%capacity == front
Circular queue uses modulo arithmetic for pointer wrapping; Linear queue only increments forward
Both have O(1) enqueue, but dequeue is O(n) in linear (shifting) vs O(1) in circular
Circular queue is more memory efficient and preferred in OS for scheduling and buffering

questions.question: Write a C program to implement a circular queue using an array with enqueue, dequeue, and display operations.
keyPoints: Define array and front/rear variables initialized to -1
Enqueue: check full using (rear+1)%MAXSIZE == front; handle first insertion setting both pointers to 0; use modulo for rear increment
Dequeue: check empty using front==-1 && rear==-1; handle last element removal by resetting both to -1; use modulo for front increment
Display: traverse from front to rear using modulo to handle wrap-around
Include proper overflow (Queue Overflow) and underflow (Queue Underflow) error messages

questions.question: List and explain any three real-world applications of circular queues.
keyPoints: CPU Scheduling (Round Robin): OS manages processes in cyclic order with time slices
Traffic Light Systems: cycle through signals in fixed circular order (Red→Green→Yellow→Red)
Printer Spooling: print jobs added to queue and processed in order with efficient buffer management
Producer-Consumer Problem: shared buffers in concurrent programming; streaming data buffers for audio/video

questions.question: Trace the contents of a circular queue of capacity 5 after performing the following operations in sequence: Enqueue(10), Enqueue(20), Enqueue(30), Enqueue(40), Dequeue(), Dequeue(), Enqueue(50), Enqueue(60), Enqueue(70). Show the array contents, front, and rear after each operation.
keyPoints: After Enqueue(10): front=0, rear=0, array:[10|----|----|----|----]
After Enqueue(20): front=0, rear=1, array:[10|20|----|----|----]
After Enqueue(30): front=0, rear=2, array:[10|20|30|----|----]
After Enqueue(40): front=0, rear=3, array:[10|20|30|40|----]
After Dequeue(): front=1, rear=3, array:[-|--|30|40|----] (10 removed)
After Dequeue(): front=2, rear=3, array:[-|--|--|40|----] (20 removed)
After Enqueue(50): front=2, rear=4, array:[-|--|--|40|50]
After Enqueue(60): front=2, rear=0 (wrap), array:[60|--|--|40|50]
After Enqueue(70): front=2, rear=1 (wrap), array:[60|70|--|40|50] - Queue now FULL

questions.question: Explain the concept of circular increment using modular arithmetic. How does the formula (index + 1) % capacity achieve wrap-around behavior? Give examples for capacity = 5.
keyPoints: Modulo operator (%) returns remainder after division, creating periodic wrapping behavior
When index reaches capacity-1, adding 1 and applying %capacity returns 0
Examples: (4+1)%5=0 (wraps from index 4 to 0); (3+1)%5=4 (normal increment); (0+1)%5=1 (normal increment)
This formula is used for both front and rear pointer updates during enqueue and dequeue operations
Wrap-around allows reusing empty slots at the beginning after dequeuing elements
